coredata.dc:title: The ability of carbon nanoparticles to increase transmembrane current of cations coincides with impaired synaptic neurotransmission
coredata.dc:creator: Shatursky, Oleg Ya.
Demchenko, Alexander P.
Panas, Ihor
Krisanova, Natalia
Pozdnyakova, Natalia
Borisova, Tatiana
coredata.dc:description: Here, carbon nanodots synthesized from β-alanine (Ala-CDs) and detonation nanodiamonds (NDs) were assessed using (1) radiolabeled excitatory neurotransmitters L-[14C]glutamate, D-[2,33H]aspartate, and inhibitory ones [3H]GABA, [3H]glycine for registration of their extracellular concentrations in rat cortex nerve terminals; (2) the fluorescent ratiometric probe NR12S and pH-sensitive probe acridine orange for registration of the membrane lipid order and synaptic vesicle acidification, respectively; (3) suspended bilayer lipid membrane (BLM) to monitor changes in transmembrane current. In nerve terminals, Ala-CDs and NDs increased the extracellular concentrations of neurotransmitters and decreased acidification of synaptic vesicles, whereas have not changed sufficiently the lipid order of membrane. Both nanoparticles, Ala-CDs and NDs, were capable of increasing the conductance of the BLM by inducing stable potential-dependent cation-selective pores. Introduction of divalent cations, Zn2+ or Cd2+ on the particles` application side (cis-side) increased the rate of Ala-CDs pore-formation in the BLM. The application of positive potential (+100 mV) to the cis-chamber with Ala-CDs or NDs also activated the insertion as compared with the negative potential (−100 mV). The Ala-CD pores exhibited a wide-range distribution of conductances between 10 and 60 pS and consecutive increase in conductance of each major peak by ~10 pS, which suggest the clustering of the same basic ion-conductive structure. NDs also formed ion-conductive pores ranging from 6 pS to 60 pS with the major peak of conductance at ~12 pS in cholesterol-containing membrane. Observed Ala-CDs and NDs-induced increase in transmembrane current coincides with disturbance of excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitter transport in nerve terminals.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Planar lipid bilayers
Carbon nanodots
Detonation nanodiamonds
Ion-selective pores
Synaptic neurotransmission

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: An increase in balancing extracellular concentrations of excitatory neurotransmitters L-[14C]glutamate (A) and D-[2,33H]aspartate (B) and inhibitory neurotransmitters [3H]GABA (C) and [14С]glycine (D) in nerve terminal preparations in the presence of Ala-CDs and NDs at different concentrations. The data are presented as the mean ± SEM. *, p < 0.05; **, p < 0.01; ***, p < 0.001 as compared to the control; n = 12.

Changes in F560/F610 ratio of NR12S probe in nerve terminals in response to application of Ala-CDs at different concentrations. A – Typical normalized spectra of NR12S fluorescence with subtraction of synaptosome autofluorescence; B – Calculated F560/F610 ratio of NR12S probe, where each measurement was done in 4 records; n = 12. The data are presented as the mean ± SEM. C – Dose-response curves: Effects of Ala-CDs (0.05–0.5 mg/ml) and NDs (0.05–0.5 mg/ml) on acridine orange (AO) fluorescence. An increase in the dye fluorescence after application of the nanoparticles at the steady state level was considered as 100% in calculations.

Typical current-time traces induced by the interaction of Ala-CDs with PC/cholesterol suspended planar bilayers (weight ratio of 2:1) and amplitude histogram of Ala-CD-induced single pore conductances. Membrane separated solution contained 100 mM KCl. Currents were induced by the addition of Ala-CDs at a final concentration of 4 μg/ml and monitored under +100 mV applied on the side of BLM opposite to where Ala-CDs were introduced (cis-side). Inset (left) – Amplitude histogram of single Ala-CD-pore conductances defined under above conditions; (right) - Current measurements of voltage-clamped PC/cholesterol BLM (weight ratio of 2:1) at a holding potential of +50 mV. Solid line represents zero current.

Typical current measurements exhibiting the rate of Ala-CD-induced pore-formation at different voltages in voltage-clamped bilayer membranes of PC/cholesterol (weight ratio of 2:1) suspended in symmetrical solutions of 100 mM KCl (A) and 10 mM CaCl2 (B). The membrane potential is shown below the current trace. The particles were introduced at a final concentration of 4 μg/ml to the cis-side of membrane opposite to the voltage application side (trans-side). Inset – the activation of Ala-CD-induced pore-formation by Cd2+ (left); the lack in blocking efficiency of Cd2+ on the integral steady-state current induced by Ala-CDs. Current-time traces were measured in a symmetric 100 mM KCl solution. (↑) - Addition of cadmium chloride. Zero current is shown by a solid line.

Typical interaction of Ala-CDs with PC/cholesterol suspended planar bilayers at different weight ratios and reversal potentials assessed from current-voltage relationships under 10:1 KCl gradient and at bi-ionic conditions. A, left (far left panel) – Steadily ascending transmembrane current after exposure of PC/cholesterol BLM (weight ratio of 2.5:0.5) to Ala-CDs; (next panel) – different transient current patterns (steadily increasing current, step-like increases in persistent current) followed by exposure of PC/cholesterol BLM (weight ratio of 2:1) to Ala-CDs and potential-dependence of integral current. Applied voltages are shown below the current trace. Dotted lines margin steadily increasing component of transmembrane current. A, right – Steady-state current-voltage dependence of Ala-CD-modified PC/cholesterol BLM (weight ratio of 2:1) in symmetrical solution of 100 mM KCl. Inset, − Current measurements representing the gating of Ala-CD-induced pore in suspended PC/cholesterol BLM (weight ratio of 2:1) (upper trace) and PC/cholesterol BLM (weight ratio of 1:1) (lower trace). B, left - Steady-state current–voltage relationships of PC/cholesterol bilayers (weight ratio of 2:1) modified by Ala-CDs. The membrane separated solution contained: curve 1 — 100 mM KCl from both sides of the membrane; curve 2 — 100 mM KCl on the side of membrane to which the voltage was applied and 10 mM KCl from the opposite side of membrane. B, right - Typical dependence of PHMG-induced steady-state current across planar PC/cholesterol lipid bilayer at a weight ratio of 2:1 on the membrane potential and ionic composition of membrane bathing solution. The curves represent current-voltage relationships after consecutive replacements of 100 mM KCl for equimolar solutions of different monovalent metal chlorides from Ala-CDs insertion side.

Typical current-time traces induced by the interaction of Ala-CDs with PE/PC/PS planar bilayers (weight ratio of 1:1:0.4) suspended in 100 mM KCl solution, amplitude histogram of Ala-CD-induced single pore conductances and reversal potential assessed from current-voltage relationships at 10:1 KCl gradient. Currents were induced by the addition of Ala-CDs to the cis-side at a final concentration of 4 μg/ml and monitored under +50 mV applied to particles-free trans-side of membrane. Solid line represents zero current. Inset (left) – Steady-state current–voltage relationships of PE/PC/PS bilayers modified by Ala-CDs. The membrane separated solution contained: curve 1 — 100 mM KCl from both sides of the membrane; curve 2 — 100 mM KCl on the trans-side of membrane and 10 mM KCl from the cis-side of membrane; (right) - Amplitude histogram of single Ala-CD-pore conductances defined in a symmetrical solution of 100 mM KCl at +50 mV.

Typical current measurements exhibiting NDs-induced pore-formation at different holding potentials, ionic selectivity and potential-dependence of steady-state current across NDs-modified bilayer membrane. Lipid bilayer was composed of PC/cholesterol at a weight ratio of 2:1 and suspended in symmetrical solutions of 100 mM KCl. Currents were induced by the addition of NDs at a final concentration of 0.6 ng/ml and monitored under membrane potential of +100 mV applied to cis-side of membrane. Solid line represents zero current.

Top, left - Current-time trace induced by the interaction of NDs with suspended PC/cholesterol planar bilayers; inset - the rate of NDs-induced pore-formation at a holding potential of ±100 mV. Right - amplitude histogram of NDs-induced single pore conductances; inset – typical single pore conductances. Bottom - Current-voltage relationships of NDs-modified lipid bilayer measured in a symmetrical solution of 100 mM KCl (left) and after consecutive replacement of 100 mM KCl for 10 mM KCl from NDs insertion side. Zero current potential was assessed at 10:1 KCl gradient as a voltage at which the transmembrane current reversed (−35 mV) (right).
Capability of nanoparticles to form the ion-selective pores in the BLM corresponds to disturbance of membrane-associated neurotransmitter transport characteristics in nerve terminals.

head.ce:title: The ability of carbon nanoparticles to increase transmembrane current of cations coincides with impaired synaptic neurotransmission

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, carbon nanodots synthesized from β-alanine (Ala-CDs) and detonation nanodiamonds (NDs) were assessed using (1) radiolabeled excitatory neurotransmitters L-[14C]glutamate, D-[2,33H]aspartate, and inhibitory ones [3H]GABA, [3H]glycine for registration of their extracellular concentrations in rat cortex nerve terminals; (2) the fluorescent ratiometric probe NR12S and pH-sensitive probe acridine orange for registration of the membrane lipid order and synaptic vesicle acidification, respectively; (3) suspended bilayer lipid membrane (BLM) to monitor changes in transmembrane current. In nerve terminals, Ala-CDs and NDs increased the extracellular concentrations of neurotransmitters and decreased acidification of synaptic vesicles, whereas have not changed sufficiently the lipid order of membrane. Both nanoparticles, Ala-CDs and NDs, were capable of increasing the conductance of the BLM by inducing stable potential-dependent cation-selective pores. Introduction of divalent cations, Zn2+ or Cd2+ on the particles` application side (cis-side) increased the rate of Ala-CDs pore-formation in the BLM. The application of positive potential (+100 mV) to the cis-chamber with Ala-CDs or NDs also activated the insertion as compared with the negative potential (−100 mV). The Ala-CD pores exhibited a wide-range distribution of conductances between 10 and 60 pS and consecutive increase in conductance of each major peak by ~10 pS, which suggest the clustering of the same basic ion-conductive structure. NDs also formed ion-conductive pores ranging from 6 pS to 60 pS with the major peak of conductance at ~12 pS in cholesterol-containing membrane. Observed Ala-CDs and NDs-induced increase in transmembrane current coincides with disturbance of excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitter transport in nerve terminals.

ce:keyword.ce:text: air pollution p articulate m atter

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Widespread R&D efforts are focused over the last two decades on the investigation of new unique properties of carbon-based nanomaterials as one of the most expansively used classes of materials. Unique mechanical, optical, electrochemical, versatile surface, size, and shape properties inherent to carbon-based nanomaterials are very perspective for implementation in nanotechnologies [1]. Carbon exists in diverse allotropes, e.g. amorphous carbon, graphite, diamonds, carbon nanotubes, graphene oxide, graphene quantum dots, and fullerenes [2]. The carbon family members have been exploited in various biological applications, including drug delivery, diagnosis, cancer therapy, biosensing, tissue engineering, and bioimaging [3,4].

Carbon nanodots (CDs), allotropic carbon-based nanomaterials synthesized by heating of β-alanine (Ala-CDs) and detonation nanodiamonds (NDs), were used in this study. Besides nanotechnology, CDs obtained by heating of organics can be a model of ultrafine carbon-containing air pollution p articulate m atter (PM) emitted to the environment during natural fire hazards and anthropogenic activity. Carbon-containing nanoparticles are one of the main components of air pollution PM that now is considered to associate with nervous system disorders and neurodegenerative disease, including attention deficit/hyperactivity in children, dementia in adults, autism, and stroke, however the mechanisms of harmful effects of carbon-containing air pollution PM are still far from being clear [5–7].

Until now, numerous investigations have been carried out on the biological activity of carbon nanoparticles [8–11]. Recently, we revealed that Ala-CDs, CDs from citric acid and thiourea, and NDs reduced the initial velocity of transporter-mediated uptake and accumulation of fast amino acid excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitters, glutamate and γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA), respectively, changed the plasma membrane potential, and exocytosis in cortex nerve terminals. Effects of CDs from citric acid and thiourea in brain nerve terminals were less efficient as compared with Ala-CDs [12,13]. Nano-sized carbon-containing particles emitted during plastic combustion also provoked presynaptic malfunction through reduction of the membrane potential, synaptic vesicle acidification, and transporter-mediated uptake of glutamate and GABA in cortex nerve terminals [14]. The polarization of nerve terminal membrane regulates functioning of neurotransmitter transporters and their extracellular levels, for instance, glutamate and GABA transporters are driven by Na+/К+ electrochemical gradient across plasma membrane [15]. In turn, extracellular concentrations of neurotransmitters in nerve terminals are established by a definite balance of their transporter-mediated uptake and tonic leakage and reflect efficiency of active neurotransmitter turnover across the plasma membrane [16,17].

The ability of the nanoparticles to affect the membrane potential of nerve terminals, can be assessed by the disturbance of the membrane integrity measuring conductivity of a suspended lipid bilayer (BLM) in the presence of these nanoparticles [17]. Literature data have suggested that nanoparticles are capable to induce stable pores in lipid bilayer [18–22], where ionogenic groups of the particles and phospholipid heads take part in the formation of ion-selective site [19,23]. The inhibition of the neurotransmitter transporter functioning may result from the dissipation of the Na+/К+electrochemical gradient [13,15] or proton gradient [12] due to the current of leak appeared as a result of membrane dissolution after nanoparticles-induced lipid phase separation [18,24] and/or pore-formation [12,19–21,25].

To confirm above suggestions, Ala-CDs and NDs were assayed for membrane activity on the BLMs of different lipid compositions. Obtained results were verified by the experiments with cortex nerve terminals. Physical and chemical, surface and magnetic properties of Ala-CDs and NDs samples used in this study were thoroughly characterized in previous publications [12,15,26–29] (see Materials and methods for details). The aim of this study was to assess an ability of Ala-CDs and NDs: (1) to increase the conductance of lipid bilayer, (2) to change the lipid order in plasma membrane of the cortex nerve terminals, and (3) to misbalance the membrane-associated neurotransmitter transport processes in cortex nerve terminals. These parameters were estimated by (*) BLM technique, (**) fluorescence method of registration of the membrane lipid order and synaptic vesicle acidification using the fluorescent ratiometric and pH-sensitive probes NR12S and acridine orange, correspondingly, and (***) registration of changes in balancing extracellular concentrations of excitatory neurotransmitters L-[14C]glutamate, D-[2,33H]aspartate, and inhibitory neurotransmitters [3H]GABA, [3H]glycine in the preparations of cortex nerve terminals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Materials
ce:section.ce:para: Analytical grade metal chlorides (potassium, sodium, calcium, lithium, cesium), n-heptane, ergosterol, Tris–HCl were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemie GmbH (Steinheim, Germany). Cholesterol and 1,2-diphytanoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (DPhPC) were purchased from Avanti Polar Lipids, Alabaster, AL, USA. Phosphatidylethanolamine (PE), phosphatidylserine (PS). phosphatidylcholine (PC) were obtained from Kharkiv factory of biopreparations Biolik, Ukraine and additionally purified by column chromatography using Al2O3.

EDTA, EGTA, HEPES, aminooxiacetic acid, d-glucose, sucrose, Ficoll 400, Sigma-Fluor® High Performance LSC Cocktail, analytical grade salts were purchased from Sigma (St. Louis, MO, USA); L-[14C]glutamate, D-[2,33H]aspartate, [3H]GABA (γ-[2,3-3H(N)]-aminobutyric acid), [3H]glycine were from Perkin Elmer (Waltham, MA, USA). NR12S was kindly provided by Dr. Andrey Klymchenko (Strasbourg University, France) and Dr. Oleksandr Kucherak (Charles University, Czech Republic).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and properties of Ala-CDs and NDs
ce:section.ce:para: Ala-CDs were synthesized from β-alanine dissolved in distilled water and treated in microwave oven (600 W, 1.5 min) according to [12,26]. The obtained material was diluted with distilled water and centrifuged repeatedly (10 min × 5,000 g) before supernatant was collected. Then, the supernatant was dried until the constant weight was achieved. The stock solution at a final concentration of 7 mg/ml was prepared by dissolving dried pellet in distilled water. Obtained Ala-CDs could be additionally purified by column chromatography using Sephadex G-100 in order to rid the preparation of unreacted precursors. Ala-CDs carried weak negative charge that was shown by horizontal gel electrophoresis with 1.5% agarose gel [28]. The value of the isoelectric point, where the zeta potential crosses from positive to negative was at pH 5–6 [30]. The CDs shell was formed by amorphous carbon and oxygen-containing polar carboxyls, carbonyls and hydroxyls groups [12,26]. The size of CDs varied from 1 to 6 nm [12,26,31,32]. Ala-CDs were provided by Dr. Mariia Dekaliuk from the Department of Neurochemistry of the Palladin Institute of Biochemistry NAS of Ukraine.

NDs were obtained by detonation synthesis [27] using a detonation wave at the explosion with negative oxygen balance (trotyl/hexogen,grade TG-40/60). The unburned residue in the preparation (metals, ceramics) consisting of 4.7% [15,27]. The electrophoretic charge of the nanoparticle surface was negative [29]. Electrokinetic potential was −9.22 mV [33]. Infrared spectroscopy of NDs showed moisture in the form of bound –OH groups and free water molecules, the presence of carbonyl and carboxyl groups at the surface, whereas content of –CH2 and –CH3 groups was insignificant [34]. The size of NDs varied between 10 and 50 nm and also had larger micro-size inclusions [15,27]. Detonation nanodiamonds were provided by Dr. Olga Leshchenko from the Bakul Institute for Superhard Materials NAS of Ukraine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experiments using BLM technique: lipid bilayer formation
ce:section.ce:para: The formation and stability of the pore was assessed by constant monitoring of transmembrane current across the suspended planar lipid bilayer. The bilayer membranes were formed according to [35] from the solution of cholesterol and/or zwitterionic phospholipids: PC, PE, DPhPC in n-heptane. The weight ratios of the mixtures of cholesterol with phospholipids were maintained at 1:2, 1:1, 0.5:2.5, while the amount of phospholipids in the mixtures of PE/PC or PE/DPhPC was equal. Negatively charged PS was added to the mixture with zwitterionic phospholipids at a weight ratio of 2 PE: 1 PC or DPhPC: 0.4 PS. The total lipid concentration of every membrane-forming mixture was 20 mg/ml. Bilayer membranes were suspended in a round aperture of a Delrin cup (0.15 mm diameter, Warner Instruments, Inc., USA) with a working volume of 1 ml.

The actual bilayer (black membrane) formation was monitored in reflected light by MBS-9 binocular microscope (St. Petersburg Optico–Mechanical Plant LOMO, Russian Federation) and rectangular test pulses internally generated by BBA-02 amplifier (Eastern Scientific, LLC, Rockville, USA) connected to membrane washing saline through a HS-2 head-stage (Eastern Scientific, LLC, Rockville, USA). The membrane washing solution contained 10 mM Tris–HCl (pH 7.4) and the required quantities of metal chlorides. Both membrane separated chambers could be stirred if necessary.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transmembrane current recordings and data analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Voltage-clamp recordings of transmembrane current were made via a high-resolution amplifier BBA-02 (Eastern Scientific, LLC, Rockville, USA). The potential difference was referenced to the outer glass chamber, which was defined as being at ground potential. Voltage was applied to the saline inside Delrin cup held within a glass chamber. Membrane separated saline was connected to HS-2 head-stage of the amplifier BBA-02 through a pair of agar bridges immersed into a 2 M KCl solution with silver chloride electrodes EAG-02 (Eastern Scientific, LLC, Rockville, USA) placed on the opposite sides of suspended bilayer. The polarization between assembled electrodes was maintained at 1.5 mV. Voltage-ramp protocols of 60 mV/min or holding potentials within the range of ±100 mV were applied by universal signal generator RG-3 (Eastern Scientific, LLC, Rockville, USA) or voltage-source built in amplifier BBA-02. Transmembrane currents were filtered at 100 Hz and recorded by a computer through USB Digitizer Model ADA-1210 (Eastern Scientific, LLC, Rockville, USA) using Cole-Palmer Instrument Company software (Illinois, USA). The computer-made records were analyzed with Origin Pro 8.6.0 software. All experiments were carried out at a room temperature (20–24 °C), each made on a newly brushed membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experiments using animals: ethical approval
ce:section.ce:para: 100–120 g body weight Wistar rats, males, were kept in the animal facilities of the institute, housed in temperature-controlled quiet room at 22–23 °C, and provided ad libitum with water and dry food pellets. The experimental procedures were carried out in accordance with the guidelines of the European Community (2010/63/EU) and local laws and policies and were approved by the Animal Care and Use Committee of the Palladin Institute of Biochemistry National Academy of Sciences of Ukraine (the Protocol from September 02, 2019). The studies are reported in accordance with the ARRIVE guidelines for reporting experiments involving animals [36,37]. Rats were sacrificed before removing the brain by rapid decapitation. The number of rats used in the study was 30, i.e. the assessment of extracellular concentrations of L-[14C]glutamate, D-[2,33H]aspartate, [3H]GABA, and [3H]glycine in the nerve terminal preparations −24 animals; fluorimetry −6 animals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Isolation of rat cortex nerve terminals (synaptosomes)
ce:section.ce:para: The cortex areas of the rat brains were rapidly removed and homogenized in ice-cold 0.32 M sucrose, 5 mM HEPES-NaOH (pH 7.4), and 0.2 mM EDTA. Manipulations were performed at 4 °C. One animal was used to isolate one synaptosomal preparation, and each record was performed in triplicate. The synaptosomes were prepared by differential and density gradient centrifugation of rat brain homogenate using Ficoll-400 according to the method of Cotman with minor modifications [38–40]. The synaptosomes were used during 2–4 h after isolation. The standard saline solution was oxygenated and contained (in mM): NaCl 126; KCl 5; MgCl2 2.0; NaH2PO4 1.0; HEPES 20, pH 7.4; and d-glucose 10. Protein concentration was recorded as described by Larson [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorimetric experiments using synaptosomes
Lipid order of nerve terminal membranes
ce:section.ce:para: To stain synaptosomes with NR12S, an appropriate aliquot of the dye stock solution in DMSO was added to the standard saline solution, and after vortexing, this solution was immediately added to the synaptosomal suspension with desired Ala-CDs or NDs content to obtain final probe concentrations of 0.04 μM (<0.2% vol. DMSO). Final concentration of synaptosomes was 0.02 mg of protein/ml. The synaptosomal suspension was incubated before the measurements with the probe for 30 min in the dark at room temperature. The NR12S spectra were recorded with spectrofluorometer PTI QuantaMaster40, with excitation of 520 nm. Quantitative analysis of changes in fluorescence spectra of NR12S was performed by 560/610 nm intensity ratio.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Acidification of synaptic vesicles in nerve terminals
ce:section.ce:para:
A pH-sensitive fluorescent dye acridine orange that can be selectively accumulated by the acid compartments of nerve terminals was applied to assess acidification of synaptic vesicles. The dye fluorescence was recorded using spectrofluorometer PTI QuantaMaster40 and Hitachi at 490 nm excitation and 530 nm emission wavelengths. Acridine orange at a final concentration of 5 μM was added to synaptosomes (0.2 mg of protein/ml), pre-incubated in a stirred thermostatted cuvette at 37 °C for 10 min. Fluorescence (F) was calculated according to: F = F t /F 0 (F 0 and F
t were fluorescence intensities of the dye in the absence and presence of the synaptosomes, respectively). F 0 was calculated by extrapolation of exponential decay function to t = 0. An increase in the dye fluorescence after application of the nanoparticles at the steady state level was considered as 100% in calculations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Radiolabeled assay: extracellular L-[14C]glutamate and D-[2,33H]aspartate concentrations in nerve terminal preparations
ce:section.ce:para: The synaptosomal suspension was diluted with the standard saline solution up to 2 mg of protein/ml, and after preincubation at 37 °C for 10 min was loaded with L-[14C]glutamate (20 μM, 5 μCi/ml) or D-[2,33H]aspartate (0.45 μM, 5 μCi/ml) at 37 °C for 10 min. The suspension after loading was washed with 10 volumes of ice-cold oxygenated standard saline solution; the pellet was resuspended in the solution to a final concentration of 1 mg of protein/ml. The suspension (125 μl; 0.5 mg of protein/ml) was preincubated at 37 °C for 10 min, then the nanoparticles were added and incubated for 6 min, and then rapidly sedimented using a microcentrifuge (for 20 s at 10,000 g). Extracellular concentration of L-[14C]glutamate or D-[2,33H]aspartate in synaptosomal preparations was measured for 6 min and was recorded in aliquots of supernatants (100 μl) by liquid scintillation counting with Sigma-Fluor® High Performance LSC Cocktail (1.5 ml), and the levels were expressed as the percentage of total accumulated L-[14C]glutamate or D-[2,33H]aspartate [12]. Data were collected in triplicate from several independent experiments performed with different synaptosomal preparations (n).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extracellular [3H]GABA concentrations in nerve terminal preparations
ce:section.ce:para: The synaptosomal suspension was diluted with the standard saline solution up to 10 mg of protein/ml and after preincubation at 37 °C for 10 min was loaded with [3H]GABA (0.2 μM, 5 μCi/ml) in the standard saline solution for 10 min. 100 μM aminooxyacetic acid was present throughout the [3H]GABA experiments. The synaptosomal suspension after loading was washed with 10 volumes of ice-cold standard saline solution. The pellet was resuspended in the solution to obtain protein concentration of 1 mg of protein/ml. The synaptosomal suspension (120 μl) was preincubated at 37 °C for 10 min, then the nanoparticles were added and incubated for 5 min, and then rapidly sedimented using a microcentrifuge (20 s at 10,000 g). Extracellular [3H]GABA concentration in nerve terminal preparations was recorded at 5 min time point in aliquots of supernatants (90 μl) using liquid scintillation counting with Sigma-Fluor® High Performance LSC Cocktail (1.5 ml), and the values were expressed as the percentage of total accumulated [3H]GABA [15]. Data were collected in triplicate from several independent experiments performed with different synaptosomal preparations (n).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extracellular [3H]glycine concentrations in nerve terminal preparations
ce:section.ce:para: The synaptosomal suspension was diluted by the standard saline solution up to 10 mg of protein/ml and after preincubation at 37 °C for 10 min was loaded with [3H]glycine (45 μM, 5 μCi/ml) in the standard saline solution for 10 min. The synaptosomal suspension after loading was washed with 10 volumes of ice-cold standard saline solution. The pellet was resuspended in the solution to obtain protein concentration of 1 mg of protein/ml. The synaptosomal suspension (120 μl) was preincubated at 37 °C for 10 min, then the nanoparticles were added and incubated for 5 min, and then rapidly sedimented using a microcentrifuge (20 s at 10,000 g). The extracellular [3H]glycine concentration in nerve terminal preparations was recorded at 5 min time point using scintillation counting as described in the previous subsections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statistical analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Results were expressed as the mean ± S.E.M. of n independent experiments. The difference between two groups was compared by one-way ANOVA. Differences were considered significant, when p < 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results

Radiolabel assay: an increase in the extracellular concentrations of neurotransmitters L-[14C]glutamate, D-[2,33H]aspartate, [3H]GABA, [3H]glycine in nerve terminal preparations after administration of Ala-CDs and NDs
ce:section.ce:para: Balancing extracellular concentrations of excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitters were measured using nerve terminals preliminary loaded with radiolabeled excitatory neurotransmitters L-[14C]glutamate and [3H]aspartate, and inhibitory ones [3H]GABA and [3H]glycine (see Method section). It was revealed that both Ala-CDs and NDs increased in a dose-dependent manner (starting from a concentration of 0.5 mg/ml) extracellular concentrations of L-[14C]glutamate (Fig. 1A), D-[2,33H]aspartate (Fig. 1B), [3H]GABA (Fig. 1C), and [3H]glycine (Fig. 1D) in nerve terminal preparations. Increased extracellular concentrations of above neurotransmitters in nerve terminal preparations in the presence of Ala-CDs and NDs indicated changed efficiency of active turnover of excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitters across the plasma membrane, and disrupted neurotransmitter transport.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorimetric assay
The lipid order of nerve terminals after administration of Ala-CDs and NDs
ce:section.ce:para: To analyze whether a nanoparticle-induced increase in the extracellular levels of neurotransmitters in nerve terminal preparations shown in the previous subsection (Fig. 1) was associated with changes in the lipid order of the nerve terminal membrane, the following fluorimetric experiments were performed. The effect of Ala-CDs and NDs on the lipid order of synaptosome membranes was investigated using a NR12S fluorescent ratiometric probe [42]. The concentration dependence of the F560 / F610 ratio of NR12S probe that reflects the state of the membrane lipid environment in the presence of Ala-CDs in the synaptosomal suspension has been measured. All samples were monitored in 3 repeats for each Ala-CDs sample and for control samples lacking Ala-CDs. Fluorescence intensities of blank samples were subtracted from related NR12S - stained samples in order to make correction for synaptosome autofluorescence. It was revealed that Ala-CDs did not change the lipid order of synaptosome membranes. Therefore, Ala-CDs did not disorder the synaptosome lipid bilayer within the concentration range 100–400 μg/ml (Fig. 2 ). NDs at final concentrations of 5, 10, 15 μg/ml did not have any effects on the lipid order of the synaptosome membranes. An increase in a ND concentration up to 400 μg/ml (similar to Ala-CDs) was impossible because of low probe transparency and limitation of the method. Therefore, an increase in the extracellular levels of neurotransmitters in nerve terminal preparations after nanoparticle administration was not due to disordering of nerve terminal membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: A decrease in synaptic vesicle acidification after administration of Ala-CDs and NDs
ce:section.ce:para: Another fluorimetric dye was applied to analyze a cause of a nanoparticle-induced increase in the synaptosomal extracellular levels of the neurotransmitters shown in Fig. 1. A synaptic vesicle acidification (as a component of the electrochemical proton gradient) was monitored using a рН-sensitive fluorescent dye acridine orange [43]. Application of both Ala-CDs and NDs caused an increase in the dye fluorescence, thereby reflecting а decrease in acidification of synaptic vesicles (Fig. 2 C), that in turn can increase the synaptosomal extracellular levels of the neurotransmitters (Fig. 1). One of the reasons of а decrease in acidification of synaptic vesicles may be nanoparticle-induced disturbance of membrane integrity and dissipation of the proton gradient of synaptic vesicles driven by V-ATPase following by the release of their neurotransmitter content to the cytoplasm of the nerve terminals, and then to the extracellular space.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BLM assay
Pore-formation induced by Ala-CDs in BLMs with different lipid compositions
ce:section.ce:para:
A nanoparticle-induced increase in the extracellular levels of neurotransmitters in nerve terminal preparations may result from dissipation of the membrane potential of nerve terminals caused by the nanoparticles, and so changes in neurotransmitter transporter functioning. Consequently, in the next set of experiments, the conductance of lipid bilayer was assayed using BLM technique in the presence of nanoparticles. The exposure of PC/cholesterol, DPhPC/cholesterol, PE/PC/PS and PE/DPhPC/PS BLM kept at different weight ratios to Ala-CDs was followed by a variety of transmembrane current patterns indicative of pore-formation (Figs. 3–6
). These include steadily increasing current (Fig. 5A, left), transient current spikes (Figs. 5A, 6) and relatively stable step-like increases in current interrupted by brief falls within a fluctuating diameter of each separate step, which suggest rapid resealing of the single pore (Figs. 3, 5A–6). The mean unitary conductance of stable single-pore events in PC/cholesterol and DPhPC/cholesterol BLM varied in magnitude within the range of 10 to 60 pS at a holding potential of 100 mV, with approximately 10 pS distance between major peaks on the amplitude histogram (Fig. 3, left inset). Similar distribution of stable single-pore conductance events is also represented in an amplitude histogram obtained at 2-fold lower voltage, 50 mV applied to the same side of PC/cholesterol, DPhPC/cholesterol and PE/PC/PS membrane (Fig. 6, right inset).

The pore-formation occurred in the symmetrical membrane-washing water solutions of mono- and divalent cations (NaCl, KCl and CaCl2) at 50 mV and 100 mV voltages applied on the cis-side of membrane to which Ala-CDs were added, regardless to the sign of the membrane potential (Fig. 3, Fig. 3, right insert, Fig. 5A, B). This suggests that the magnitude and the sign of membrane potential are not the necessary prerequisites for Ala-CD-induced pore-formation although, positive holding potential (+100 mV) applied to the cis-side of membrane obviously facilitated the creation of transmembrane ion-conductive pores as compared to the negative holding potential (−100 mV) (Fig. 4A,B).
In all experiments voltages were applied to the Ala-CDs-free trans-side of membrane. Ala-CDs were introduced to a cis-side at a final concentration of 4 μg/ml. Solid line represents zero current.

The application of divalent cations such as Zn2+ and Cd2+ to Ala-CDs-containing side of membrane at a final concentration of 5 mM also increased the rate of pore-formation in a symmetrical 100 mM KCl bathing solution (Fig. 4A, left inset). Increasing amount of stable, lack in complete resealing pores eventually formed a steady-state integral current consisting of many Ala-CD-pores. Alternatively, lasting Ala-CD-induced increase in current was stopped by flushing Ala-CDs-containing compartment with particles-free saline and also remained stable indefinitely for all types of Ala-CD-modified BLMs. This suggests irreversible interaction of Ala-CDs with sterol-containing or sterol-free charged and neutral phospholipid bilayers (Figs. 3–6).

Although Cd2+ and Zn2+ increased the rate of Ala-CD pore-formation, these cations did not affect the summary steady-state conductance after addition of 5 mM Cd2+ or Zn2+ to the side of Ala-CDs insertion after the pores had been formed in a symmetrical solution of 100 mM KCl (Fig. 4A, right inset).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential-dependence and ionic selectivity of Ala-CD pores
ce:section.ce:para: The current-voltage relationships and ionic selectivity of Ala-CDs-created current across BLMs of all studied lipid compositions were determined by the application of a voltage ramp as the current reached a steady-state level (Fig. 5A, right, B, Fig. 6, left inset). The effect of applied voltage gradients on magnitude of current flowing across Ala-CD-modified bilayer was also explored by switching the holding potentials between ±100 mV (Fig. 4, Fig. 5A, left). Hence, a similarity of amplitude histograms obtained at a holding potential of +100 mV (Fig. 3, left inset), and + 50 mV (Fig. 6, right inset) can be explained by almost linear current-voltage dependence at positive voltages applied to the trans-side opposite to where Ala-CDs were added (Fig. 5A, right). This implies that at 2-times lower holding potential, the major conductance peaks are reduced approximately by a factor of 2 (Fig. 3, left inset, Fig. 6, right inset) for all types of BLMs tested.

The application of steadily increasing negative voltages rectified the steady-state Ala-CDs-induced summary current, raising 1.2–1.5 times more current at −100 mV than at +100 mV applied to the Ala-CDs-free chamber (Fig. 5A). Obtained coefficient of asymmetry suggests slight prevalence of inward current of cations across Ala-CD-induced pores at positive potentials on the application side that corresponds to the physiological situation, where Ala-CD-pores are formed from extracellular side.

The Nernst potential obtained for steady-state Ala-CDs-induced current in the membrane washing saline of 10 mM KCl on the trans-side and 100 mM KCl on the cis-side of PC/cholesterol membrane consisted of −16.7 ± 2.0 mV (Fig. 5 B, left). Therefore, the relative permeability ratio (PK+ /PCl −) estimated according to Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz Eq. [44] was 2.27 ± 0.13 that suggests a weak cation selectivity of Ala-CD-pores. The ion selectivity of Ala-CD-pores in chloride solutions of different monovalent cations K+, Cs+, Na+ and Li+ was determined by the reversal potentials of stationary current at bi-ionic conditions against 100 mM KCl (Fig. 5B, right). The symmetrical solution of 100 mM KCl was replaced from the cis-side by the required solution of another metal chloride after the transmembrane current in a symmetric solution of 100 mM KCl reached its steady-state. The relative permeabilities calculated for 100 mM KCl against 100 mM CsCl, 100 mM NaCl and 100 mM LiCl were 0.79 ± 0.02, 1.47 ± 0.02 and 1.86 ± 0.06, correspondingly as the permeability of K+ in the symmetrical 100 mM KCl solution was assigned as 1. The selectivity sequence for monovalent metal chlorides obtained on Ala-CD-modified PC/cholesterol membrane (Cs+ > K+ > Na+ > Li+) fits within first two Eisenman sequences and, thus, represents a weak binding site for cations [44–46].

Placing 10 mM CaCl2 vs. 10 mM KCl on the opposite sides of 2PC (or DPhPC) / 1 cholesterol BLM did not shift the zero current potential measured for Ala-CD-pores in a symmetrical solution of 10 mM KCl (data not shown). This coincides favorably with the low univalent cation-selectivity of persistent Ala-CD-pores and inability of the other divalent cations, Cd2+ or Zn2+ to affect steady-state Ala-CD-induced transmembrane current in the solution of 100 mM KCl (Fig. 4A, right inset).

Although the addition of negatively charged PS to sterol-free PE/PC (or DPhPC) BLM did not change the conventional pattern of Ala-CD-induced stable conductance events (Fig. 3, left inset, Fig. 6, right inset), it increased the magnitude of the voltage at which the steady-state Ala-CD-induced integral current reversed under conditions of 10-fold KCl concentration gradient from −16.7 ± 2.0 mV to −29 ± 4 mV measured on Ala-CD-modified PC/cholesterol BLM and Ala-CD-modified PE/PC/PS BLM, respectively (Fig. 5 B, left, Fig. 6, left inset). This also raised the relative permeability ratio (PK + /PCl −) from 2.27 ± 0.13 of Ala-CD-modified PC/cholesterol BLM to 4.35 ± 0.15 of Ala-CD-modified PE/PC/PS BLM.

The change in PC (or DPhPC)/cholesterol weight ratio in lipid bilayer composition had no appreciable influence neither on the conductance nor on ionic selectivity of Ala-CD-pores. Instead, it affected the lifetime of Ala-CD-induced pores as it occurred with amphotericin B pores formed in PC (or DPhPC)/cholesterol BLMs at different weight ratios [47–49].

The Ala-CD-pores incorporated into the PC (or DPhPC)/cholesterol BLM maintained at a weight ratio of 2 PC (or DPhPC)/1 cholesterol exhibited long-lasting openings interrupted by both long (up to 30 s and more) and short (seconds and less) closures (Fig. 5A right, inset, upper trace).

Increase in cholesterol/PC or cholesterol/DPhPC weight ratio decreased the lifetime of some Ala-CD-induced pores dramatically since cholesterol/PC or DPhPC weight ratio rose from 0.5 to 1 (Fig. 5A right, inset, lower trace). While the long openings had been reduced, Ala-CDs-modified BLM exhibited short-duration transient current spikes within a fluctuating diameter of single pore, where most of the open pore current events lasted seconds and less. Conversely, decrease in cholesterol/PC or cholesterol/DPhPC weight ratio to 0.2 almost excluded the step-like increases and brief current spikes from a pattern of transient currents leaving only steadily ascending current in membrane-separated solutions of 100 mM KCl, 100 mM NaCl, and 10 mM CaCl2 (Fig. 5A, far left panel). The potential-dependence and ionic selectivity of stationary Ala-CD-induced current across BLM maintained at 0.5 cholesterol/1 DPhPC (PC) or 1 cholesterol/1 DPhPC (PC) weight ratios (data not shown) were similar with those measured in PC (or DPhPC)/cholesterol BLMs composed at 2:1 (Fig. 5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the ion-conductive properties of ND-induced pores in BLMs
ce:section.ce:para: The exposure of PC/cholesterol at different weight ratios (2:1, 1:1, 2.5:0.5) and PE/PC (1:1) BLM to NDs resulted in stable step-like increases in current interrupted by brief falls within a fluctuating diameter of some of the steps indicative of pore-formation and rapid resealing (Fig. 7 , top, left, right, inset). Despite the domination of persistent transmembrane ion flow without complete membrane resealing, the patterns of transient currents also included brief current spikes (Fig. 7, top, left, right, inset). The mean unitary conductance of stable single-pore events in PC/cholesterol BLM varied in magnitude within the range of ~6 to 80 pS at a holding potential of 100 mV with the major peak at approximately 12 pS (Fig. 7, top, right).

Although the ND-induced pore-formation occurred in BLMs of all lipid compositions tested at holding potentials of different sign and magnitude (data not shown), much faster insertion rates were achieved at positive voltage applied to the cis-side of BLM (Fig. 7, top, left, inset).

The integral steady-state ND-induced current was plotted versus voltage after flushing the particles-containing chamber with ND-free saline. Application of steadily increasing negative voltages on particles-free trans-side of modified BLM slightly rectified the current rising 1.5–2.0 times more current at −80 mV than at +80 mV (Fig. 7, bottom, left). The reversal potential of steady-state ND-induced current in the membrane separated solutions of 10 mM KCl on the application side of the particles and 100 mM KCl on the opposite side of PC/cholesterol membrane was −35 ± 2.0 mV (Fig. 7, bottom, right). The relative permeability ratio (PK+ /PCl−) calculated by Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz Eq. [44] consisted of 6.5 ± 2.1, which suggests that the current flowing across ND-pores is mainly comprised of cations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion
ce:section.ce:para: In classical studies of ion-selective pore-forming proteins, there is a highly conserved sequence of amino acid residues and quaternary protein structure that leads to the appearance of typical reproducible single-pore conductance events, ionic selectivity, current-voltage dependence, etc. The interaction of nanoparticles Ala-CDs and NDs with bilayer membranes of different lipid compositions also results in a variety of single-pore conductance events. Although the mean unitary conductance of stable Ala-CD single-pore events in PC/cholesterol, DPhPC/cholesterol and PE/PC/PS membrane varied in magnitude, the distance between major peaks on the amplitude histogram was similar (~10 pS) (Figs. 3, 6). This may result from clustering of the same ion-conductive Ala-CD-induced pores, similarly to other reported cases [50]. The single ion-conductive Ala-CD pore or cluster of several pores can be assembled (*) in the membrane-washing solution, or (**) on the surface of lipid bilayer as a pre-pore complex before the nanopores have to be trapped in membrane, or (***) directly within the core of lipid bilayer as described for the pore-forming proteins and nanoparticles [23,25,50–53].

The assessment of neurotoxicity carried out using isolated rat brain nerve terminals suggested that Ala-CDs and NDs can affect the membrane-associated processes of transporter-mediated uptake and release of excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitters [12,13,15]. The linear dose-response of [3H]GABA and L-[14C]glutamate release obtained at different concentrations of Ala-CDs [12] with the slope of nearly 1 in log-log coordinates suggests that one ion-conductive Ala-CD-nanopore or cluster of the pores can be formed by single pre-pore Ala-CD particle [21,54] or aggregate of several Ala-CD particles/nanopores [23,25,51]. An Ala-CD/ND-induced increase in balancing extracellular level of L-[14C]glutamate, D-[2,33H]aspartate, [3H]GABA, and [3H]glycine (Fig. 1) in nerve terminal preparations may result from dissipation of the ionic electrochemical gradients caused by these nanoparticles assembled into a single unit either in the membrane washing saline or on the surface of lipid bilayer before the penetration through a lipid bilayer or partitioning into the hydrophobic core of the bilayer essential enough to cause the ionic leakage across the membrane [18,25]. According to the estimations carried out using ratiometric fluorescent probe NR12S the interaction of Ala-CDs or NDs with the membrane has failed to reduce the orderly state of lipid bilayer (Fig. 2 A, B). It can be speculated that the nanoparticle-induced local rearrangement in the lipid functional groups of the nerve terminal membrane during pore formation may not necessarily lead to sufficient disorder of the lipid bilayer. Hence, the increased amount of neurotransmitters in extracellular space is likely to result from dissipation of the proton gradient across vesicular membrane through the regular pore-like Ala-CD/ND-created ion-conductive structures (Figs. 3, 7 top, left) without appreciable contribution of ionic leakage via disordered bilayer. Ala-CD/ND-induced pores may also be involved in the release of neuromediators from non-vesicular pool. Besides, nanoparticles can be accumulated by synaptic vesicles during vesicular recycling. This may reduce synaptic vesicle acidification (Fig. 2 C) by dissipation of the proton gradient across vesicular membrane through Ala-CD/ND-pores created from inside. It should be noted that synaptic vesicle membrane is enriched with cholesterol. Conversely, the neurotransmitters can be released through the utilization of the synaptic vesicles own transportation machinery due to the excessive amount of nanoparticles in their inner space. All three mechanisms suggest nanoparticle-induced inhibition of nerve terminals ability to keep neurotransmitters inside intracellular compartments that eventually elevates the ambient concentration of neurotransmitters outside the nerve cell.

Apart from fluctuating step-like increases in current, Ala-CDs also created steadily ascending current in PE/PC/PS (data not shown), PC/cholesterol and DPhPC/cholesterol BLMs (Fig. 5A, left). Contribution of Ala-CD-induced steadily increasing current component becomes dominant within the temporal (brief current spikes vs. persistent current) and overall pattern of transient currents observed on modified BLM with lower cholesterol/PC or cholesterol/DPhPC weight ratio of 0.25 (Fig. 5A, left). Perhaps, the lack in consecutive step-like increases that would represent the formation of detectable unitary pores (Figs. 3, 5) was a consequence of decrease in concentration of membrane cholesterol resulted in reduction of lipid bilayer phase segregation caused by cholesterol [55,56]. Hence, less orderly packed membrane lipids spread the pattern of cholesterol-induced “wells” that hinders the partitioning of Ala-CD particles and their following aggregation into a pre-pore complex on the surface of 2.5 PC (DPhPC) /0.5 cholesterol BLM, thus reducing the size and conductance of resulting ion-conductive pores [57]. This is consistent with previous observations that single channel-like persistent current transitions from open to close states were caused by individual particles on sterol-free 1,2-dioleoyl-sn-3-phosphatidylcholine (DOPC) membrane [21].

The decrease in PC (DPhPC) /cholesterol weight ratio reduced the lifetime of Ala-CD-induced pores. Since PC (DPhPC) /cholesterol weight ratio changed from 2 (molar ratio 1 for PC/cholesterol and 0.9 for DPhPC/cholesterol) to 1 (molar ratio 0.5 for PC/cholesterol and 0.42 for DPhPC/cholesterol) some of the pores exhibited short-duration current bursts (each opening lasted seconds and less (Fig. 5A, right insert) that looked similar to gating of non-toxic amphotericin B channels in cholesterol-containing phospholipid bilayers [47,48]. Indeed, such a dramatic reduction of Ala-CD-pore lifetime is likely to diminish the Ala-CD-induced dissipation of ionic gradients across membranes and, therefore, neurotoxicity caused by membrane-associated release and uptake of excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitters [12]. The cholesterol/phospholipid molar ratio appreciably greater than 1 takes place in mammalian cell plasma membrane in pathological conditions, while normally it is below the value of 1 [47,49]. For instance, in plasma membranes of rat liver cells it consists of 0.58 [58]. Thus, the small conductance Ala-CD/ND-pores probably make the most appreciable contribution to the process of plasma membrane modification (Figs. 3A, left, 7 top, left), unless the gradual increase in current is induced by the other modes of interaction with membrane [21,25,54]. Interestingly, minimal concentrations of Ala-CDs and NDs, which changed transport across the synaptosome plasma membrane of neurotransmitters L-[14C]glutamate, D-[2,33H]aspartate, [3H]GABA, and [3H]glycine (Fig. 1), were 100 times higher than pore-forming ones in BLM experiments (Figs.3–7).

In this study, the exposure of BLMs of all lipid compositions tested to Ala-CDs induced transmembrane pores. Hence, neither longer 18‑carbon acyl chains of most common oleic and linoleic acids nor shorter 16‑carbon tails of palmitic acid and 14‑carbon tails of myristic acid in PC-containing BLMs or 16‑carbon tails of 3,7,11,15-tetramethylhexadecanoic acid in DPhPC-containing BLMs prevented the pore-formation. This seems particularly important for modification of bilayers suspended in a water phase as, regardless to the lipid content, these membranes are approximately 1.25–2-times thicker than the native or solvent-free membranes [47,59] that may prevent the creation of nanopores [47,48,60]. Unsaturated chains in 9-cis-octadecenoic, oleic and linoleic acid residues that could potentially enhance insertion of Ala-CDs to the lipid bilayer due to decreasing number of hydrophobic interactions between phospholipid acyl tails of the lipid bilayer core [23] also had no significant influence on the interaction with membrane because the rate of pore-formation in PC-containing BLMs (PC/cholesterol, PE/PC/PS) was similar with that of DPhPC-containing BLMs (DPhPC/cholesterol, PE/DPhPC/PS) (Figs. 3 and 6).

Reliable discrete pore-formation by Ala-CDs and NDs was observed in cholesterol-reach BLM at 1:1 and 2:1 PC/cholesterol weight ratios. Perhaps, sterol-enhanced mechanism of membrane adsorption described in [57] could facilitate nanoparticles pore assembly and penetration into a cholesterol-containing BLMs due to the pattern of cholesterol-induced “wells” on the surface of lipid bilayer capable of accommodating nanoparticles at the distances appropriate for their aggregation into a pre-pore complex. This is proved by the formation of much larger ion-conductive structures following conventional small conductance (10–15 pS) Ala-CD and ND pores in cholesterol-containing membranes (Figs. 3, 7).

Although cholesterol increased the rate of Ala-CD-/ND-induced pore-formation and reduced lifetime of Ala-CD-pores the exposure of sterol-free bilayer membranes (PE/PC, PE/DPhPC, PE/PC/PS and PE/DPhPC/PS) to all particles tested also induced pores. The presence of sterol is, therefore, not a necessary prerequisite to Ala-CD- and ND- induced pore-formation. Thus, the perturbations of the polar headgroups and the hydrophobic core of phospholipid bilayers followed by application of Ala-CDs and NDs to the membrane lead to the formation of cation-selective transmembrane pores (Figs. 3, 6–7) regardless to the presence of sterol, length or saturation of phospholipid acyl chain and overall membrane thickness.

The actual formation of Ala-CD- and ND-pores does not require acidic lipids as the particles-induced step-like increases in current occurred on uncharged membranes comprised of zwitterionic lipid (Figs. 3–7). Apparently, anionic phospholipids and membrane sterol accumulate the particles on the membrane surface until reaching a threshold concentration necessary for the formation of ion-conductive pores [12,23,57], while electrostatic repulsion facilitates the following penetration of lipid bilayer by predominantly positively charged particles after the application of positive voltage on the particles application side (Figs. 4, 7). This is relevant to the physiological conditions, where Ala-CD- and ND-induced pores are formed from extracellular side and proves previous reports that small-sized positively charged nanoparticles are more toxic than negatively charged nanoparticles because of much higher potency at the insertion into the lipid bilayer [19,21].

The formation of Ala-CD-induced pores in the solutions of univalent cations, such as 100 mM KCl, appears to be enhanced in the presence of divalent cations on the cis-side of membrane where Ala-CDs were introduced. The increase in transmembrane current observed after the addition of CdCl2 to the same side of the membrane occurred because of an increased rate of incorporation and, possibly, the increased conductance of single ion-conductive pores in the bilayer membrane (Fig. 4A, left inset). The addition of CdCl2 to free of Ala-CDs trans-side did not affect Ala-CD-induced pore-formation. However, it is still possible that an interaction between the divalent cations and the BLM increases the rate of pore-formation and single pore conductance, when divalent cations and Ala-CDs are present on the same side of membrane as this property of divalent cations has been proven to facilitate the fusion of some pore-forming proteins with lipid bilayers [61,62]. Alternatively, the increase in the separate Ala-CD-pore conductance caused by the divalent cations may result from a structural change of the pore itself. Even though the reasons for the enhancement of Ala-CDs-induced pore-formation and conductance of the single pores by divalent cations remain obscure, the presence of physiologically significant divalent cations on the same side of the membrane with Ala-CDs can be important for its pore-forming activity in vivo as shown for pore-forming toxins [62,63].

The formation of long-open and, thus, toxic Ala-CD- and ND-induced pores is supposed to cause influx of Na+ as these cation-selective structures pass more current at positive voltage applied to the side of membrane where the particles were introduced (Figs. 5A, right, 7 bottom, left). The Ala-CD-pore formed on extracellular side may also induce efflux of K+ or influx of Ca2+ because it is weakly preferential to K+ (PK +/PNa + = 1.47 ± 0.02) and does not discriminate between Ca2+ and univalent cations. The activation effect of a negative potential on ionic transport across modified BLMs and unideal preference of Ala-CD- and ND-pores to cations vs. anions suggest that the negative charge inside putative target-cells at the rest state can also stimulate efflux of chloride driven by Ala-CD- and ND-pores after insertion from the extracellular space.

Taking into account the modes of pore-formation proposed for antimicrobial macromolecules and proteins [23,45,52] it is possible to suggest that charges responsible for ionic selectivity of nanoparticles-induced pore are contributed by the ionogenic groups of their shells (hydroxyls, carboxyls, etc.) and membrane phospholipid headgroups, particularly phosphonic acid residues as Ala-CD- and ND-pores are cation-selective [19]. Hence, slight increase in relative permeability ratio (PK+/PCl−) from 2.27 ± 0.13 of Ala-CD-modified neutral PC/cholesterol (weight ratio of 2:1) BLM to 4.35 ± 0.15 of Ala-CD-modified PE/PC/PS (weight ratio of 1:1:0.4) BLM (Figs. 5B, left, 6, left insert) can be explained by enhancement of negative charge of Ala-CD shells lining the hydrophilic interior of the pore after inclusion of negatively charged carboxyls and phosphonic acid residues of PS heads [19,23]. The inability of CdCl2 or ZnCl2 to block Ala-CD–induced integral conductance across neutral PC/cholesterol BLM washed by sodium (data not shown) and potassium chlorides (Fig. 4A, right inset) may, then, result from much stronger interactions between K+ or Na+ versus Cd2+ or Zn2+ with unbound carboxyls of Ala-CDs and phosphoric acid residues of zwitterion PE within Ala-CD pore's selective filter [23]. There is also a possibility that the oppositely charged functional groups in the selectivity site of Ala-CD pores are mere inaccessible to zinc and cadmium ions.

It is known that nano-sized CDs can be a part of carbon-containing air pollution PM emitted to the environment during fires and anthropogenic activity. It should be noted that ultrafine PM0.1 (PM with size less than 0.1 μm) is more hazardous to human health as compared to fine PM2.5 and coarse PM10 [64]. From environmental point of view, capability of carbon-containing air pollution PM to form ion-selective pores may cause non-reversible changes in cellular membranes thus underlying harmful health effects of this PM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism of acute Ala-CD- and ND-induced neurotoxicity via the formation of potential-dependent cation-selective pores in the plasma membranes of the nerve cells was suggested. Nanoparticles, Ala-CDs and NDs, form pores from extracellular side at the rest state and induce transmembrane current of physiologically significant ions that dissipates electrochemical gradients across the plasma membrane of the target-cell, thereby affecting transporters of neurotransmitters in the nerve terminals. This results in increased extracellular concentration of neurotransmitters, L-[14C]glutamate, D-[2,33H]aspartate, [3H]GABA, [3H]glycine. Thus, nanoparticle-induced change in BLM conductivity can be an adequate approach to predict acute toxicity.

Although similar selectivity and potential-dependence of Ala-CD- and ND-induced pores suggest common mechanisms of cytotoxicity there are differences in spatial structure of Ala-CD- and ND-pores, the charge and accessibility of their binding sites for cations that determine diversity of unitary pore conductance, relative permeability to different ions and potential-dependence of stationary summary currents. Besides negatively charged groups present in the shells of Ala-CD and ND, cationic selectivity of Ala-CDs depends on the negative charge of phospholipid heads, as the addition of PS to the mixture of zwitterions PC, DPhPC and PE increased selectivity of the pores for positive potassium ions vs. chloride.

The ability of Ala-CDs and NDs to form the ion-selective pores in the BLM rather corresponded to disturbance of excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitter transport and synaptic vesicle acidification, than the orderly state of lipid bilayer in nerve terminals (Scheme 1 ).
